abstract: Scaling inference-time compute has emerged as an important driver of LLM performance, making inference efficiency a central focus of model design alongside model quality. While the current Transformer-based models deliver strong model quality, their quadratic compute and linear memory make inference expensive. This has spurred the development of sub-quadratic models with reduced linear compute and constant memory requirements. However, many recent linear models trade off model quality and capability for algorithmic efficiency, failing on tasks such as state tracking. Moreover, their theoretically linear inference remains hardware-inefficient in practice. Guided by an inference-first perspective, we introduce three core methodological improvements inspired by the state space model (SSM) viewpoint of linear models. We combine: (1) a more expressive recurrence derived from SSM discretization, (2) a complex-valued state update rule that enables richer state tracking, and (3) a multi-input, multi-output (MIMO) formulation for better model performance without increasing decode latency. Together with architectural refinements, our Mamba-3 model achieves significant gains across retrieval, state-tracking, and downstream language modeling tasks. At the 1.5B scale, Mamba-3 improves average downstream accuracy by 0.6 percentage points compared to the next best model (Gated DeltaNet), with Mamba-3's MIMO variant further improving accuracy by another 1.2 points for a total 1.8 point gain. Across state-size experiments, Mamba-3 achieves comparable perplexity to Mamba-2 despite using half of its predecessor's state size. Our evaluations demonstrate Mamba-3's ability to advance the performance-efficiency Pareto frontier.

citations.fullCitation: [18] Albert Gu and Tri Dao. Mamba: Linear-Time Sequence Modeling with Selective State Spaces. 2024. arXiv: 2312.00752 [cs.LG]. url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.00752.
citations.justification: This paper introduced the original Mamba model and the concept of selective state spaces. Mamba-3 is a direct successor that builds upon and aims to improve the expressivity and capabilities of this foundational architecture.
citations.title: Mamba: Linear-Time Sequence Modeling with Selective State Spaces

citations.fullCitation: [11] Tri Dao and Albert Gu. Transformers are SSMs: Generalized Models and Efficient Algorithms Through Structured State Space Duality. 2024. arXiv: 2405.21060 [cs.LG]. url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.21060.
citations.justification: This work, which introduced Mamba-2, is the immediate predecessor to Mamba-3. It established the State Space Duality (SSD) framework that connects SSMs to attention, a concept heavily referenced and built upon in the Mamba-3 paper. Mamba-3's contributions are explicitly framed as improvements on Mamba-2.
citations.title: Transformers are SSMs: Generalized Models and Efficient Algorithms Through Structured State Space Duality

citations.fullCitation: [19] Albert Gu, Karan Goel, and Christopher Ré. Efficiently Modeling Long Sequences with Structured State Spaces. 2022. arXiv: 2111.00396 [cs.LG]. url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2111.00396.
citations.justification: This is the seminal S4 paper that introduced structured state space models (SSMs) for sequence modeling. The Mamba family of models, including Mamba-3, are direct descendants of this foundational line of research and evolve the principles established in S4.
citations.title: Efficiently Modeling Long Sequences with Structured State Spaces

citations.fullCitation: [46] Jianlin Su, Yu Lu, Shengfeng Pan, Ahmed Murtadha, Bo Wen, and Yunfeng Liu. RoFormer: Enhanced Transformer with Rotary Position Embedding. 2023. arXiv: 2104.09864 [cs.CL]. url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2104.09864.
citations.justification: This paper introduced Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE). Mamba-3 leverages a data-dependent version of the 'RoPE trick' for the efficient implementation of its novel complex-valued state, which is one of the paper's three core contributions for improving state-tracking capabilities.
citations.title: RoFormer: Enhanced Transformer with Rotary Position Embedding

intermediateReport:
## Detailed Report: Mamba-3: Improved Sequence Modeling using State Space Principles

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research paper "Mamba-3: Improved Sequence Modeling using State Space Principles" was authored by:

*   Aakash Lahoti (Carnegie Mellon University)
*   Kevin Y. Li (Carnegie Mellon University)
*   Berlin Chen (Princeton University)
*   Caitlin Wang (Princeton University)
*   Aviv Bick (Carnegie Mellon University)
*   J. Zico Kolter (Carnegie Mellon University)
*   Tri Dao (Princeton University, Together AI)
*   Albert Gu (Carnegie Mellon University, Cartesia AI)

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

The development of Large Language Models (LLMs) has primarily relied on Transformer-based architectures, which have demonstrated substantial capabilities. However, these models inherently feature quadratic computational complexity and linear memory requirements (due to the KV cache) during inference. This characteristic makes inference operations computationally expensive, particularly as models scale in size and context length. The increasing emphasis on inference-time compute for unlocking new LLM capabilities, such as chain-of-thought reasoning and agentic workflows, has intensified the demand for more efficient model designs.

In response to these limitations, a significant research direction has focused on developing sub-quadratic models, including State Space Models (SSMs) and linear attention mechanisms. These models aim to reduce computational costs to linear time and maintain constant memory requirements during inference, while ideally preserving or improving model quality. Recent advancements, such such as Mamba-2 and Gated DeltaNet (GDN), have seen these linear layers integrated into large-scale hybrid models, demonstrating performance comparable to pure Transformer architectures with enhanced efficiency.

Despite these successes, existing linear models face several challenges. Some models trade off model quality and specific capabilities, such as robust state tracking (e.g., tasks like determining parity of bit sequences), for algorithmic efficiency. Furthermore, while theoretically efficient, their inference algorithms can exhibit low hardware efficiency in practice. This often stems from their design being optimized primarily for training, leading to memory-bound decoding phases with low arithmetic intensity on modern hardware. This work, Mamba-3, aims to address these identified limitations by enhancing both the model's capabilities and its practical inference efficiency, thereby seeking to advance the performance-efficiency Pareto frontier for sequence models.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objective of this research is to advance the Pareto frontier between model quality and inference efficiency in sub-quadratic sequence models. This overarching goal is driven by several specific motivations and translates into three core methodological improvements:

1.  **Addressing Inference Efficiency Bottlenecks**: The increasing reliance on inference-time compute in LLM applications, such as complex reasoning tasks and agentic systems, highlights the limitations of Transformer architectures. Their quadratic compute and linear memory usage (due to the KV cache) during decoding translate to high costs and slow response times. The motivation is to design an architecture that is inherently efficient for deployment-scale inference, moving beyond the theoretical linear scaling of prior sub-quadratic models to achieve practical hardware efficiency. This includes addressing the low arithmetic intensity observed in current linear model decoding.

2.  **Enhancing Model Quality and Capabilities**: While previous linear models have achieved efficiency gains, some have exhibited trade-offs in model quality or specific capabilities. For instance, Mamba-2 was developed to prioritize training speed and simplicity, which in some cases resulted in reduced expressivity compared to Mamba-1 when considering inference-matched models. A notable limitation across many current linear models is their inadequate performance on state-tracking tasks, such as specific formal language problems requiring "rotational" hidden state dynamics. The objective is to imbue the model with richer expressive power to handle such tasks without significantly increasing computational burden.

3.  **Formalizing and Optimizing Foundational Components**: Previous state space model implementations, particularly in the context of linear time-varying (LTV) systems like Mamba-1 and Mamba-2, utilized discretization heuristics that lacked complete theoretical justification. A motivation behind this work is to provide a formal framework for discretizing LTV SSMs and to leverage this framework to derive more expressive and accurate recurrence relations. Additionally, there is a motivation to refine architectural components to synergize with the core SSM updates, potentially simplifying the overall model structure while improving performance.

To achieve these objectives, Mamba-3 introduces three methodological pillars:
*   A more expressive recurrence derived from a refined SSM discretization technique.
*   A complex-valued state update rule designed to enhance state-tracking capabilities.
*   A multi-input, multi-output (MIMO) formulation intended to improve hardware utilization and overall model performance during inference.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

Mamba-3 introduces three core methodological improvements, each rooted in an SSM-centric viewpoint, along with architectural refinements.

#### 4.1. Exponential-Trapezoidal Discretization

**Background**: State Space Models are fundamentally continuous-time systems that require discretization for processing discrete sequence data. Earlier LTV SSMs, such as Mamba-1 and Mamba-2, employed a heuristic discretization method that approximated the state-input integral with a first-order Euler rule after an exponential integration. This approach lacked explicit theoretical justification and had an O(Δ²) local truncation error.

**Mamba-3's Approach**: This work introduces a general framework for discretizing LTV SSMs, which formalizes the heuristic used in Mamba-1/2 as "exponential-Euler." Building upon this, Mamba-3 proposes a new "exponential-trapezoidal" discretization method.
*   **Derivation**: The method begins by analyzing an exponential-adjusted system, separating the approximation of the state-transition integral (using a right-hand approximation) from the state-input integral.
*   **Recurrence Formula**: For the state-input integral, the exponential-trapezoidal rule applies a second-order accurate approximation, yielding a three-term recurrence: h_t = α_t h_{t-1} + β_t B_{t-1} x_{t-1} + γ_t B_t x_t. Here, α_t = exp(Δ_t A_t), β_t = (1 - λ_t)Δ_t exp(Δ_t A_t), and γ_t = λ_t Δ_t, where λ_t ∈ [0,1] is a data-dependent scalar (parameterized as σ(u_t)). This provides a local truncation error of O(Δ³).
*   **Expressivity**: This three-term recurrence is characterized as an implicit width-2 convolution applied to the state-input *within* the core recurrence. This internal convolution is posited to contribute sufficient expressivity to potentially obviate the need for an explicit short causal convolution layer, which is commonly used in other linear models.
*   **Parallel Representation**: The exponential-trapezoidal recurrence is shown to fit within the Structured State Space Duality (SSD) framework. Its parallel form corresponds to a structured mask composed of a 1-semiseparable matrix and a 2-band matrix, which facilitates hardware-efficient parallel computation during training.

#### 4.2. Complex-Valued State Space Models

**Background**: To achieve efficiency, prior modern SSMs (e.g., Mamba-1/2) simplified the state-transition matrix to have real, typically non-negative, eigenvalues. This simplification, however, has been shown to restrict the models' capabilities on specific state-tracking tasks that require "rotational" hidden state dynamics, such as parity functions, which are often solvable by LSTMs but not by prior real-valued linear models.

**Mamba-3's Approach**:
*   **Reintroduction of Complex States**: Mamba-3 reintroduces complex-valued SSMs. A complex-valued SSM with state dimension N/2 is demonstrated to be mathematically equivalent to a real-valued SSM with doubled state dimension (N), where its transition matrix is a scalar-decayed block-diagonal matrix of 2x2 data-dependent rotation matrices.
*   **Data-Dependent RoPE Equivalence**: This real-valued formulation is further shown to be equivalent to applying data-dependent Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) to the input (B) and output (C) projection components of a vanilla scalar-transition SSM. The cumulative product of these rotation matrices across time steps transforms the B and C parameters.
*   **Implementation**: This "RoPE trick," adapted from existing techniques, enables efficient implementation of complex-valued state transitions with minimal computational overhead compared to real-valued SSMs. This design aims to provide the necessary expressive power for state tracking without significant performance degradation.

#### 4.3. Multi-Input, Multi-Output (MIMO) SSM

**Background**: A key challenge in the practical deployment of linear models is that while their theoretical inference complexity is low, the decoding phase can be memory-bound with low arithmetic intensity (e.g., Mamba-2's arithmetic intensity is around 2.5 ops/byte). This leads to underutilization of GPU compute resources.

**Mamba-3's Approach**:
*   **Motivation**: To increase hardware utilization during decoding, Mamba-3 transitions from a single-input, single-output (SISO) recurrence to a multi-input, multi-output (MIMO) formulation.
*   **Mechanism**: In SISO Mamba, the state update H_t ∈ R^(N x P) involves an outer product (B_t ∈ R^N, x_t ∈ R^P) with FLOPs proportional to N*P. In MIMO, the input projection B_t is expanded to R^(N x R) and the input vector x_t to R^(P x R), where R is the MIMO rank. The state update then becomes a hardware-efficient matrix-matrix multiplication (B_t X_t^T).
*   **Benefits**: This change increases the FLOPs by a factor of R without a proportional increase in memory traffic (dominated by state H_t for small R), thereby boosting the arithmetic intensity. This aims to shift the operation from memory-bound to more compute-bound, improving tensor core utilization and allowing for more expressive computation at potentially similar wall-clock decoding latency.
*   **Training**: MIMO SSMs can be conceptualized as R² SISO SSMs, allowing existing SISO training algorithms to be adapted. A chunked algorithm is employed to reduce the training cost to R times that of SISO SSMs, rather than R².
*   **Parameterization**: To manage parameter growth, Mamba-3's MIMO uses a specific parameterization. Instead of an R-fold increase in all projections, shared B and C parameters are directly expanded, while head-specific inputs (x_t, z_t) are projected into R dimensions by element-wise scaling with learnable, data-independent vectors.

#### 4.4. Mamba-3 Architecture Refinements

The overall Mamba-3 architecture follows a pre-norm design, alternating Mamba-3 blocks with SwiGLU blocks, similar to Llama models.
*   **Updated SSM Recurrence**: Integrates the complex-valued exponential-trapezoidal SSM as the core layer.
*   **BC / QK Normalization**: RMS normalization layers (termed BCNorm or QKNorm) are added immediately after the B and C projections. This technique, mirroring practices in Transformers, is shown to stabilize large-scale training runs, enabling the removal of a post-gate RMSNorm layer present in Mamba-2.
*   **B, C Biases**: Learnable, head-specific, channel-wise biases are introduced to both B and C components after BCNorm. These biases, along with the exponential-trapezoidal discretization, are hypothesized to contribute to convolution-like behavior, empirically negating the need for an explicit short causal convolution.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The empirical validation of Mamba-3 was conducted across language modeling, retrieval tasks, state-tracking synthetics, and inference efficiency benchmarks.

#### 5.1. Language Modeling Performance

*   **Model Quality**: Mamba-3 (SISO) consistently outperforms Mamba-2 and Gated DeltaNet (GDN) across all evaluated model scales (180M, 440M, 880M, 1.5B) on average downstream language modeling accuracy. For instance, at the 1.5B scale, Mamba-3 (SISO) achieved an average downstream accuracy improvement of 0.6 percentage points over GDN, which was the next best model.
*   **MIMO Enhancement**: The Mamba-3 (MIMO) variant, using a rank R=4, demonstrated further improvements in modeling capabilities. At the 1.5B scale, MIMO added another 1.2 percentage points of accuracy, resulting in a total 1.8 percentage point gain over GDN and 1.9 points over Mamba-2. It also achieved a perplexity gain of 0.11 on 1.5B models, accompanied by a downward shift in validation loss during pretraining.
*   **Convolution Replacement**: Mamba-3 achieves strong performance without an explicit short causal convolution layer, a component often considered important in other high-performing linear models. Ablation studies indicated that the combination of BC biases and exponential-trapezoidal discretization contributed to this effect.

#### 5.2. Retrieval Capabilities

*   **Real-world Tasks**: Mamba-3 demonstrates competitive performance on real-world associative recall and question-answering tasks (e.g., TriviaQA, NQ, SQuAD). However, its performance on information extraction from semi-structured or unstructured data (SWDE, FDA) was lower compared to some baselines.
*   **Synthetic State Tracking (NIAH)**: On synthetic Needle-in-a-Haystack (NIAH) tasks, Mamba-3 showed improved out-of-distribution retrieval abilities compared to Mamba-2, particularly at context lengths exceeding its training setup.
*   **Hybrid Architectures**: When integrated into hybrid models (interleaving Mamba-3 with 5:1 ratio of linear layer to self-attention), Mamba-3 variants outperformed pure Transformer baselines. The inclusion of a pre-gate, grouped RMSNorm in hybrid Mamba-3 SISO models was found to significantly improve length generalization in NIAH tasks, albeit with a minor trade-off in in-context real-world retrieval.

#### 5.3. SSM Methodology Ablations

*   **Discretization and Bias**: Ablation studies on core SSM components at the 440M scale indicated that the combination of BC biases and exponential-trapezoidal discretization was effective. This combination allowed Mamba-3 to achieve a pretraining perplexity of 15.72, outperforming a variant without bias and trapezoidal discretization (16.68 ppl) and a variant with an explicit short convolution (15.85 ppl).
*   **Complex-Valued State Tracking**: The data-dependent RoPE embedding, resulting from the complexification of the SSM state, enabled Mamba-3 to solve synthetic state-tracking tasks that other models, including Mamba-2 and Mamba-3 without RoPE, could not. Mamba-3 achieved 100% accuracy on Parity and 98.51% on Modular Arithmetic (without brackets), and 87.75% on Modular Arithmetic (with brackets). This performance was competitive with the state-tracking-enabled GDN variant.

#### 5.4. Inference Efficiency to Performance Trade-off

*   **Pareto Frontier Improvement**: Mamba-3 (SISO) demonstrated an improved Pareto frontier for pretraining perplexity versus state size (a proxy for inference speed) compared to Mamba-2. This indicates that Mamba-3 achieves better perplexity at a given state size or comparable perplexity at a reduced state size (e.g., Mamba-3 with state size 64 matched Mamba-2 with state size 128).
*   **MIMO Advantage**: Mamba-3 (MIMO) further shifted this Pareto frontier downwards, indicating stronger modeling performance without increasing the state size or the associated decode latency.

#### 5.5. Fast Mamba-3 Kernels

*   **Decode Latency**: Optimized kernels for Mamba-3 demonstrated competitive or superior per-token decode latency. Mamba-3 (SISO) achieved the lowest latency (0.156 ms) compared to Mamba-2 (0.203 ms) and GDN (0.257 ms) at BF16 with a state size of 128.
*   **MIMO Efficiency**: Mamba-3 (MIMO, R=4) showed minimal additional latency (0.179 ms) compared to SISO, suggesting that its increased decoding FLOPs did not significantly translate into higher wall-clock time.
*   **End-to-End Latency**: Benchmarks of prefill and prefill+decode latency across varying sequence lengths showed Mamba-3 (SISO) as the fastest among recurrent models, with Mamba-3 (MIMO) performing comparably to Mamba-2. All recurrent models exhibited more favorable scaling with context length than the Transformer baseline (vLLM Llama-3.2-1B), which was affected by KV-cache overhead.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

The introduction of Mamba-3 represents an advancement in the field of sequence modeling, particularly for models designed with inference efficiency in mind. Its significance and potential impact can be considered across several dimensions:

*   **Advancing the Performance-Efficiency Pareto Frontier**: Mamba-3 demonstrates that it is possible to achieve improved model quality (lower perplexity and higher downstream accuracy) while simultaneously maintaining or enhancing inference efficiency. This is a critical development for the practical deployment of large language models, where the cost and speed of inference are often limiting factors. By offering better performance at a given inference speed or comparable performance at higher speeds, Mamba-3 can lead to more economical and responsive AI systems.

*   **Enhanced Model Capabilities**: The reintroduction of complex-valued state transitions, implemented through data-dependent Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE), addresses a known limitation of previous linear models: their inability to robustly handle state-tracking tasks. This enhancement expands the repertoire of problems that linear sequence models can effectively solve, potentially enabling them to tackle more complex logical and arithmetic reasoning tasks previously thought to be beyond their scope.

*   **Improved Hardware Utilization for Inference**: The Multi-Input, Multi-Output (MIMO) formulation is a direct response to the practical challenge of low hardware utilization during the memory-bound decoding phase of linear models. By increasing the arithmetic intensity of state updates without proportionally increasing memory traffic or decode latency, MIMO Mamba-3 can extract more computational value from modern GPU architectures. This design principle may inform future architectural optimizations in other linear-time models, leading to more efficient use of hardware resources.

*   **Architectural Simplification and Theoretical Grounding**: The exponential-trapezoidal discretization method provides a more rigorous theoretical foundation for time-varying SSMs, formalizing previous heuristics. Furthermore, the empirical finding that this, coupled with B and C biases, can obviate the need for an explicit short causal convolution layer simplifies the overall model architecture. This reduction in architectural complexity, while maintaining or improving performance, could streamline future research and development in linear sequence models.

*   **Catalyst for Future Research and Development**: Mamba-3's "inference-first perspective" and its reliance on a pure State Space Model viewpoint offer alternative design principles compared to frameworks based on linear attention or test-time regression. These distinct motivations could inspire new directions for exploring the capabilities and efficiencies of sequence models. The release of optimized kernels also facilitates easier adoption and further experimentation by the broader research community.

In conclusion, Mamba-3 contributes to the ongoing evolution of efficient sequence models by delivering a model that exhibits strong empirical performance, enhanced capabilities, and practical inference efficiency. Its methodological innovations open avenues for developing more capable and resource-efficient AI systems.
overview:
## The Evolution of Sequence Modeling Efficiency

The current landscape of Large Language Models (LLMs) is dominated by the Transformer architecture. While Transformers have demonstrated remarkable scaling properties and capabilities, they are characterized by a fundamental computational bottleneck: the self-attention mechanism requires quadratic time and memory relative to the sequence length. As researchers push toward longer context windows and more complex "agentic" workflows—where models perform iterative reasoning—the cost of maintaining the Key-Value (KV) cache during inference becomes a significant limitation.

To address this, a subset of research has focused on sub-quadratic models, most notably State Space Models (SSMs). These models, such as Mamba-1 and Mamba-2, offer linear scaling during training and constant-sized states during inference. However, transitioning from the high-capacity Transformer to more efficient SSMs often involves trade-offs. Some models struggle with specific logic-heavy tasks like state tracking, while others, despite their theoretical efficiency, fail to fully utilize modern hardware like GPUs during the inference phase. Mamba-3 is designed to bridge these gaps by refining the mathematical foundations of SSMs and optimizing their execution for modern hardware.

## Core Principles of State Space Models

At their core, State Space Models are inspired by continuous-time systems described by differential equations. An SSM maps an input signal $x(t)$ to an output signal $y(t)$ through a hidden state $h(t)$. The system is typically defined by:

$$
h'(t) = A(t)h(t) + B(t)x(t)
$$

$$
y(t) = C(t)h(t)
$$

In a digital context, these continuous equations must be "discretized" to process sequences of discrete tokens. The quality of this discretization determines how well the model captures long-range dependencies and complex patterns. Previous iterations like Mamba-1 and Mamba-2 used a simplified discretization heuristic known as "exponential-Euler." While effective for training speed, this method introduces a local truncation error of $O(\Delta^2)$, where $\Delta$ is the step size (often data-dependent).

Mamba-3 introduces a more rigorous framework for these linear time-varying (LTV) systems. It proposes an "exponential-trapezoidal" discretization. Instead of approximating the state-input integral with a simple first-order rule, Mamba-3 uses a second-order accurate approximation. This results in a three-term recurrence relation for the hidden state:

$$
h_t = \alpha_t h_{t-1} + \beta_t B_{t-1} x_{t-1} + \gamma_t B_t x_t
$$

In this formulation, the parameters $\alpha_t$, $\beta_t$, and $\gamma_t$ are derived from the underlying continuous system parameters. Specifically, $\alpha_t = \exp(\Delta_t A_t)$, while $\beta_t$ and $\gamma_t$ weight the contributions of the previous and current inputs. This method reduces the local truncation error to $O(\Delta^3)$, providing a more precise representation of the continuous dynamics.

## Enhancing Expressivity with Complex-Valued States

A persistent challenge for real-valued SSMs is "state tracking." Certain tasks require the model to maintain a count or a rotation-like state (e.g., determining the parity of a bit string or performing modular arithmetic). Earlier SSMs simplified the transition matrix $A$ to have real eigenvalues to maximize efficiency, but this effectively prevents the hidden state from "rotating," limiting its ability to solve these formal language problems.

Mamba-3 reintroduces complex-valued states to overcome this. Mathematically, a complex-valued SSM with a state dimension of $N/2$ is equivalent to a real-valued SSM with dimension $N$ that uses $2 \times 2$ rotation matrices. Rather than implementing full complex arithmetic—which can be slow—Mamba-3 utilizes a "RoPE trick." This involves applying data-dependent Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) to the $B$ (input) and $C$ (output) projections. 

The cumulative effect of these rotations across time steps allows the model to track cyclical information. This allows Mamba-3 to solve synthetic tasks like "Parity" and "Modular Arithmetic" with near-perfect accuracy, a feat that real-valued models like Mamba-2 struggle to achieve.

## MIMO: Solving the Inference Bottleneck

While SSMs are theoretically efficient because they avoid the quadratic KV cache, their practical performance on GPUs is often limited by memory bandwidth. During the "decoding" phase (generating one token at a time), the GPU spends more time moving data (the hidden state $h_t$) from memory to the processing cores than it does performing actual math. This is known as being "memory-bound," and it results in low arithmetic intensity.

Mamba-3 introduces a Multi-Input, Multi-Output (MIMO) formulation to address this. In a standard Single-Input, Single-Output (SISO) SSM, the state update is an outer product between a vector $B_t$ and a scalar/vector $x_t$. In the MIMO version, the input $x_t$ is expanded into a matrix $X_t$ with rank $R$. The state update then becomes a matrix-matrix multiplication:

$$
H_t = \alpha_t H_{t-1} + B_t X_t^T
$$

By increasing the rank $R$, the model performs significantly more floating-point operations (FLOPs) per byte of data moved. Since the hidden state $H_t$ dominates memory traffic, increasing the math performed on that state allows the GPU to utilize its "Tensor Cores" more effectively. This shift moves the operation from being memory-bound toward being compute-bound, allowing for a more expressive model without a proportional increase in wall-clock latency during inference.

The following conceptual Python snippet illustrates the difference in the state update logic for a MIMO system:

```python
import torch

def mamba3_mimo_update(h_prev, x_t, A_t, B_t, delta_t, rank_r):
    """
    Conceptual MIMO state update for Mamba-3.
    h_prev: (batch, dim_n, rank_r) - the previous state
    x_t: (batch, dim_p, rank_r) - the multi-rank input
    A_t: (batch, dim_n) - the state transition parameter
    B_t: (batch, dim_n, rank_r) - the expanded input projection
    delta_t: (batch, dim_n) - the step size
    """
    # Compute the discretization factor
    alpha = torch.exp(delta_t * A_t).unsqueeze(-1) # (batch, dim_n, 1)
    
    # MIMO Update: Matrix-Matrix Multiplication (B_t @ X_t.T)
    # This increases arithmetic intensity compared to a vector outer product.
    input_contribution = torch.matmul(B_t, x_t.transpose(-1, -2)) 
    
    # New state
    h_t = alpha * h_prev + input_contribution
    return h_t
```

## Architectural Refinements and Normalization

Beyond the core SSM logic, Mamba-3 incorporates several architectural refinements to ensure stability at scale and to simplify the model structure. 

1.  **Removal of Explicit Convolutions:** Most modern linear models (including Mamba-1/2) include a short 1D causal convolution before the SSM layer to help the model perceive local context. Mamba-3 demonstrates that the combination of the exponential-trapezoidal discretization and the introduction of learnable biases for the $B$ and $C$ projections provides enough "convolution-like" expressivity to remove the explicit convolution layer entirely. This simplifies the architecture and reduces the number of distinct operations per layer.
2.  **BC/QK Normalization:** To stabilize training, Mamba-3 adds RMS normalization layers immediately after the $B$ and $C$ projections (similar to Query-Key normalization in some Transformer variants). This helps prevent activation spikes during large-scale pretraining, which was a known issue in earlier recurrent models.
3.  **Hybrid Potential:** While Mamba-3 is a powerful standalone model, the paper also explores hybrid configurations where Mamba-3 blocks are interleaved with a small number of Attention blocks (e.g., a 5:1 ratio). These hybrids combine the efficient long-range modeling of SSMs with the precise retrieval capabilities of Attention, often outperforming pure Transformers of equivalent size.

## Empirical Findings and Performance

Mamba-3 was evaluated against several strong baselines, including Mamba-2, Gated DeltaNet (GDN), and standard Transformers (Llama-style). The results highlight several key achievements:

*   **Language Modeling Accuracy:** At the 1.5 billion parameter scale, Mamba-3 (SISO) outperformed Mamba-2 and GDN in average downstream accuracy. When the MIMO variant (rank $R=4$) was used, the performance gap widened further, achieving a 1.9 percentage point gain over Mamba-2.
*   **Pareto Frontier:** The research maps the trade-off between model quality (perplexity) and inference speed (state size). Mamba-3 consistently sits on a better Pareto frontier than its predecessors, meaning it can achieve the same level of intelligence as previous models while running faster, or better intelligence at the same speed.
*   **State Tracking Mastery:** In synthetic benchmarks designed to test a model's ability to maintain internal logic (like the Parity task), Mamba-3 solved the problems with near 100% accuracy. This validates the effectiveness of the complex-valued/RoPE-based state transitions.
*   **Hardware Efficiency:** Despite the added mathematical complexity of the trapezoidal rule and MIMO, optimized CUDA kernels for Mamba-3 achieve lower per-token decode latency than Mamba-2 and GDN. This demonstrates that mathematical sophistication does not have to come at the cost of practical speed if the implementation is hardware-aware.

## Significance in the Research Landscape

Mamba-3 represents a step forward in the quest to move beyond the Transformer's quadratic scaling. By refining the discretization process, it brings the discrete model closer to its continuous mathematical ideals. By reintroducing complex dynamics through efficient RoPE-based rotations, it fixes a core weakness in state tracking. Finally, by introducing the MIMO formulation, it acknowledges the reality of modern hardware, ensuring that theoretical linear efficiency translates into practical wall-clock speed. 

These contributions offer a blueprint for developing sequence models that are not only efficient for long-context applications but also robust enough to handle the logical and arithmetic demands of modern AI tasks. As the field moves toward more autonomous and reasoning-heavy systems, the "inference-first" design philosophy of Mamba-3 provides a viable path for scaling AI capabilities sustainably.

keyInsights: Rigorous discretization of LTV SSMs, specifically the exponential-trapezoidal method, yields more expressive recurrences that can implicitly handle convolutional aspects, potentially removing the need for explicit short causal convolutions.

Reintroducing complex-valued states, realized through data-dependent Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE), is key for linear models to robustly solve state-tracking tasks that require rotational hidden state dynamics.

Adopting a Multi-Input, Multi-Output (MIMO) formulation in SSMs effectively increases arithmetic intensity during inference, leading to higher hardware utilization and improved practical efficiency without significant latency overhead.
originalProblem: Transformer-based LLMs incur quadratic computational complexity and linear memory usage during inference, leading to high costs and slow response times at scale and long context lengths.
Prior sub-quadratic models often compromise model quality or specific capabilities like robust state tracking for algorithmic efficiency.
Existing linear model inference algorithms frequently exhibit low hardware efficiency due to memory-bound decoding phases and low arithmetic intensity.
results: Mamba-3 models consistently surpass Mamba-2 and GDN in language modeling quality, with the MIMO variant (R=4) achieving a 1.8 percentage point accuracy gain over GDN at the 1.5B scale.

The model demonstrated robust performance on synthetic state-tracking tasks, achieving 100% accuracy on Parity and 98.51% on Modular Arithmetic, a capability enabled by its complex-valued state mechanism.

Optimized Mamba-3 kernels recorded the lowest per-token decode latency among recurrent models (0.156 ms for SISO at 128 state size) and exhibited superior scaling with context length compared to Transformer baselines.
solution: Introduces an "exponential-trapezoidal" discretization method for LTV SSMs, providing a more expressive three-term recurrence with O(Δ³) truncation error.
Reintegrates complex-valued states via data-dependent Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) to enhance the model's capacity for complex state-tracking tasks.
Develops a Multi-Input, Multi-Output (MIMO) SSM formulation to improve hardware utilization and arithmetic intensity during decoding, moving from memory-bound to compute-bound operations.
summary.summary: Mamba-3 introduces a State Space Model architecture featuring exponential-trapezoidal discretization, complex-valued states, and a multi-input, multi-output formulation to improve sequence modeling. It demonstrates superior language modeling quality and enhanced inference efficiency compared to prior linear models like Mamba-2 and GDN, achieving a 1.8 percentage point accuracy gain over GDN at the 1.5B scale and lower decode latency.

title: Mamba-3: Improved Sequence Modeling using State Space Principles
